summary: Joel Spolsky pushes back against Steve McConnell's call for government-type certification of software engineers, as proposed in McConnell's book 'After the Gold Rush.' While acknowledging McConnell's previous work as excellent, Spolsky finds the certification idea deeply misguided. He cites Tom DeMarco's critique that certification primarily benefits the certifiers, not society. Spolsky drives his point home by noting that Shawn Fanning, who created Napster at 18, would never have been certified under such a scheme. He concludes bluntly: don't buy the book.
key_points: Government-style certification of programmers would serve the interests of certifiers more than the profession or society
Some of the most impactful software has been written by people who would never pass formal certification (e.g., 18-year-old Shawn Fanning writing Napster)
Even respected authors like McConnell can advocate for fundamentally wrong-headed ideas
Professionalism in software engineering cannot be reduced to credentialing
money_quotes: Certification is not something we implement for the benefit of the society but for the benefit of the certifiers.
Would 18-year-old Shawn Fanning, who wrote Napster and changed the world, have been certified?
Don't Buy 'After the Gold Rush.' It's wrong.
The new Robert Jordan book is about to ship. Buy that instead.
key_insight: Formal certification of programmers would gate-keep the profession in ways that exclude the unconventional talent responsible for some of software's most transformative innovations.
